content.method: Mixed-methods study combining discrete-choice survey experiments presenting parents with hypothetical school-quality scenarios (holding prestige/label constant or varied) and qualitative interviews with school directors and parents; triangulate with existing enrollment data on quality-matched comprehensive versus selective schools where available.
content.question: To what extent does parental demand for selective 6/8-year tracks reflect genuine pedagogical value (peer effects, teaching quality) versus prestige/status signaling, and would this demand persist if comprehensive schools achieved equivalent measured academic outcomes?
content.why_it_matters: This question underlies the viability of the quality-investment-only proposal (TP-live-t4): if demand is prestige-driven rather than quality-driven, investing in comprehensive-school quality will not reduce demand for selective tracks or the segregation they produce, regardless of resource levels. It also bears on whether admissions or fiscal-lever reforms can succeed without addressing status competition directly.
